Test file: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-11_02-13-00_game-of-life-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.spec.ts
Failing test: "should keep a block of four cells unchanged (still life)"
Expected behavior: nextGeneration on a 2x2 block returns the same 4 cells
Current error: Expected ArrayContaining 4 block cells, received []
Implementation file: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-11_02-13-00_game-of-life-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts

The simplest approach that satisfies ALL passing tests (empty -> empty, single -> empty, two adjacent -> empty, block -> block):
- Return [] if input length < 4 (handles empty, single, two adjacent)
- Return the same cells if input length is 4 (block stays)

OR the more general approach: implement neighbor counting and Conway's rules. Choose the SIMPLEST approach that passes all 4 tests.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to Refactor after completion.

content.text: I'll analyze the current state and implement the minimal code to make the failing test pass.
The simplest implementation that passes all four tests: return the input if it has 4 cells, otherwise return empty.

content.text:
All 4 tests pass.

Green Phase Complete:
- Implementation: Added `if (cells.length === 4) return cells;` before the existing `return [];`
- Result: All tests now pass (4 passing, 4 todo)
- Approach: This is the simplest possible solution that satisfies all four active tests. The block test requires returning 4 cells unchanged; all other passing tests have fewer than 4 cells and return empty. Using a hardcoded length check is the minimal baby step — no neighbor counting or Conway's rules implementation needed yet.
